summary.what: Simplify hook enforcement: drop PreToolUse, block on pre-commit + Stop backstop
summary.why: Pre-commit blocking is universal (all git clients) and simpler than Claude Code JSON protocol negotiation; PreToolUse added complexity for marginal stacking prevention
summary.how: Removed PreToolUse handler and tests from hook_claude.go, removed dispatch case from hook_run.go, moved preToolUseCommand to legacy, added PreToolUse to retiredEvents, kept Stop as session-end backstop

notes: Deep analysis of co-committing entries with code: entry IDs use format tb_<timestamp>_<anchor-short-sha> where the SHA comes from the commit being documented. Co-committing creates a circular dependency — the entry content changes the tree hash, which changes the commit SHA, which changes the entry ID. Explored ~12 approaches including two-pass commits, predictable IDs, post-commit amendment, and deferred IDs. All added complexity or broke the clean SHA-addressable property. Conclusion: separate commits for entries is the right design. The SHA reference is a feature (entries point to exact commits), not a limitation. Pre-commit hook blocks universally across all git clients (not just Claude Code), making PreToolUse redundant. Stop hook stays as a backstop for agents that somehow bypass pre-commit.
